lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: How is computer graphics described in this lecture?
option_a: An art of drawing pictures on computer screens with the help of programming
option_b: A method of compiling source code
option_c: A way to store text data
description: Computer graphics is described as an art of drawing pictures on computer screens with the help of programming, involving computations, creation, and manipulation of data.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: Computer graphics can be regarded as a rendering tool for what?
option_a: The generation and manipulation of images
description: Computer graphics is a rendering tool for the generation and manipulation of images.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: What is the primary output device in a graphical system?
description: The primary output device in a graphical system is the video monitor.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: What is the main element of a video monitor?
description: The main element of a video monitor is the Cathode Ray Tube (CRT).

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: What does the electron gun in a CRT emit?
description: The electron gun emits a beam of electrons, known as cathode rays.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: In a CRT, what systems direct the electron beam toward specified positions on the screen?
description: The electron beam passes through focusing and deflection systems that direct it toward specified positions on the phosphor-coated screen.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: What happens when the electron beam hits the screen in a CRT?
option_a: The phosphor emits a small spot of light at each contacted position
option_b: The screen heats up and shuts off
option_c: The beam is reflected back to the gun unchanged
description: When the beam hits the screen, the phosphor emits a small spot of light at each position contacted by the electron beam.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: How does the CRT keep the picture visible?
option_a: By storing it permanently in phosphor
option_b: By redrawing the picture, directing the electron beam back over the same screen points quickly
description: The CRT redraws the picture by directing the electron beam back over the same screen points quickly.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: What are the two ways by which an object can be displayed on the screen?
description: There are two ways to display an object on the screen: random scan and raster scan.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: In a raster scan system, how is the electron beam swept across the screen?
option_b: One row at a time from top to bottom
description: In a raster scan system, the electron beam is swept across the screen one row at a time from top to bottom, turning the beam intensity on and off.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: Where is the picture definition stored in a raster scan system?
description: In raster scan, the picture definition is stored in a memory area called the refresh buffer or frame buffer.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: What does the refresh/frame buffer hold?
option_a: The set of intensity values for all the screen points
description: The refresh/frame buffer holds the set of intensity values for all the screen points, which are retrieved and painted one scan line at a time.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: In a raster scan system, each screen point is referred to as what?
description: Each screen point is referred to as a pixel (picture element) or pel.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: At the end of each scan line, where does the electron beam return?
option_a: To the center of the screen
option_b: To the left side of the screen to begin the next scan line
description: At the end of each scan line, the electron beam returns to the left side of the screen to begin displaying the next scan line.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: In random scan, where is the electron beam directed?
option_a: Across every row from top to bottom
option_b: Only to the part of the screen where the picture is to be drawn
option_c: Only to the corners of the screen
description: In random scan, the electron beam is directed only to the part of the screen where the picture is to be drawn, rather than scanning the whole screen.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: Random scan is also known by which other name?
description: Random scan is also called vector display, stroke-writing display, or calligraphic display.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: How is the picture definition stored in a random scan system?
option_a: As a set of intensity values for all screen points
option_b: As a set of line-drawing commands in an area of memory
description: In random scan, the picture definition is stored as a set of line-drawing commands in an area of memory.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: What is the memory area that stores line-drawing commands in a random scan system called?
description: The picture definition in random scan is stored in an area of memory referred to as the refresh display file.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: How many times per second are random-scan displays designed to draw all the component lines of a picture?
description: Random-scan displays are designed to draw all the component lines of a picture 30 to 60 times each second.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: What is a computer graphics user interface (GUI)?
option_b: A graphic, mouse-oriented paradigm which allows the user to interact with a computer
description: A GUI is a graphic, mouse-oriented paradigm that allows the user to interact with a computer.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: The phrase "A picture is worth a thousand words" relates to which application of computer graphics?
description: Business presentation graphics are associated with the idea that "a picture is worth a thousand words".

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: In the applications of computer graphics, what does cartography refer to?
description: Cartography as an application of computer graphics refers to drawing maps.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: Which application of computer graphics involves MRIs and CAT scans for non-invasive internal examination?
description: Medical imaging uses computer graphics for MRIs, CAT scans, etc. — enabling non-invasive internal examination.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: Flight simulators and computer-aided instruction are examples of which application of computer graphics?
description: Training applications of computer graphics include flight simulators and computer-aided instruction.

lecture: Graphics Lec 2: Basics, CRT & Scan Systems (Lec.2.pdf)
question: In computer graphics applications, engineering drawings are said to be replacing what?
description: Engineering drawings (mechanical, electrical, civil, etc.) are replacing the blueprints of the past.
